hub_subtitle: Inglês para Trabalho Remoto em Tecnologia & Quality Assurance

lesson.menu_title: Hedging Language -- Sounding Measured and Diplomatically Precise
lesson.menu_desc: Ana joins an international release standup and must flag a risky bug and question the ship date without sounding blunt -- she softens her claims, qualifies her opinions and stays diplomatically precise using hedging language -- 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 15 -- Sounding Measured and Diplomatic
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Ana Claudia Veraldi | Lesson 15 -- Hedging Language

listenings.text: Let's talk about how you sound in a meeting when you are not one hundred percent sure. Many people think that hedging -- softening what you say -- makes you sound weak. In my experience, the opposite is true. When you say 'this might be a regression' instead of 'this is a regression', you sound more measured and more credible, because you are being honest about what you actually know. Being too blunt is the real risk, especially in an international meeting. If you say 'you are wrong' or 'that will never work', you can come across as arrogant, and you shut the conversation down. A good hedge keeps the door open. But there is a limit. One hedge per sentence is usually enough. If you pile them up -- 'I think that maybe it could possibly perhaps be a small issue' -- then you sound unsure, and people stop trusting your judgment. So aim for one clear, soft claim. Say what you think, qualify it once, and let the room respond. That is how you sound both precise and diplomatic.

Hi Ana, it's Sofia from the platform team, just a quick note before the standup. I saw your message about the timeout, and honestly, the way you phrased it was perfect. I learned to soften my messages the hard way, on my first international project, when a manager told me I sounded too direct in writing. So here is my favorite tip. Instead of 'you are wrong', which shuts people down, try 'I'm not sure that's quite right'. It says the same thing, but it keeps the conversation open. And when you're not certain, say 'the logs seem to suggest', not 'the logs prove'. It's honest, and it protects you if you turn out to be wrong. One warning, though. Don't over-soften. If you hedge every single word, nobody can tell what you actually think, and that's just as unclear as being too blunt. Say what you mean, soften it once, and stop there. You're getting really good at this. See you in the standup.

extra_audio.text: Here is how Ana raised her concern without sounding blunt. First, she checked the logs and formed a tentative view of the problem. Then, she softened her message so it would not sound like an accusation. After that, she suggested one more load test instead of demanding a delay. Next, she gave a rough estimate of how long the test would take. Finally, she let the team decide, and they agreed to hold the release.
